item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Can do. Thanks for the response and the approval.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Hey man, sorry for not answering earlier. I saw this but forgot to reply. I approve the change, so go for it. However, I would like to say that I am planning of redoing this skip from the start in the near future, so perhaps it will be more or less compatible with other references.

For now, let it join the rest.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

I can't speak for everyone, but if I was the one managing the SCP Foundation's containment roster I'd want to make sure that all inscription/text based anomalies (SCP-1672, SCP-1889, SCP-2140, etc) are as far from SCP-1516 as possible, lest there be a risk of 1516 gaining the abilities of those anomalies and become even more dangerous. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1516 is to be contained in a standard humanoid containment cell at Site 49, furnished with instances of SCP-1516-1. SCP-1516 is to be provided with written words referring to food and nourishment. Under no circumstances is SCP-1516 allowed to interact with written words describing harmful and/or dangerous concepts that may cause damage to the vicinity or the subject itself. As per request, SCP-1516 has been allowed to possess an audiobook copy of the Bible.

To this:

SCP-1516 is to be contained in a standard humanoid containment cell at Site 49, furnished with instances of SCP-1516-1. SCP-1516 is to be provided with written words referring to food and nourishment. Under no circumstances is SCP-1516 allowed to interact with written words describing harmful and/or dangerous concepts that may cause damage to the vicinity or the subject itself, or text/inscription based anomalies, including SCP-1672, SCP-1889, SCP-2140. As per request, SCP-1516 has been allowed to possess an audiobook copy of the Bible.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really like this. Upvoted fiercely.

For those of you that don't know: Palabra means Word.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mainly, I enjoy this one. Some SCP objects are physical representations or embodiments of concepts or ideas, and this one is the embodiment of the idea of onomatopoeia, or words describing themselves.

+1

EDIT: Onomatopeople.
I couldn't resist saying it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great idea man. Proof that the Written word is mightier than the sword. Then again, he probably would be fighting with it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It can hear you. Its senses perceive in the same way ours do… More or less.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At first I thought it was a bit too goofy to be a regular SCP but it really grew on me as I kept reading. I don't know, maybe it's just well written but there's something endearing about it. Of course, it's definitely original.

I do have one question though: how does one communicate with SCP-1516? Do you have to write down what you want to say or can it actually hear?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm very on the fence with this one, but I like it just enough to upvote. It's a bit of a strange idea, and could easily have become silly, but you managed to run with it in the right way that it isn't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this for a whole bunch of reasons.
1. You made a linguistic SCP item that specifies the language in question. And it is not English. This is not common, from what I've seen, and I like it.
2. "Weighty" concepts, like Wishun said
3. Word balloons, like Wishun said
4. You did a great job of holding my immersion; I didn't even realize the reference to the "gods" was meta until it was mentioned at the bottom of Page 1 of the discussion.
5. The line in the containment procedures about providing "written words referring to food and nourishment" made me do a double-take when first reading the article. I also initially thought it was going to be a bad Cassie knockoff, but then suddenly, instead of a 2-D man, it was a 3-D man made of text.
6. This man is literally made of words. Language and the concepts associated were always intriguing to me, although I never found it particularly enjoyable, but this is enjoyable to me. I love the concept here. The more I think about it, the more interesting it is. It leaves me with lots of questions, but it leaves me with all the right ones.

Fun fact: I tried to learn Spanish when I was in… Kindergarten I think. Using a computer program. Didn't help a lot, and the computer crashed a lot, and eventually I gave up. Still, I find the subtle connotations of language interesting.

I wonder, is this guy sophisticated enough to recognize the differences between, for example, "Junk", "Trash", "Garbage", and "Rubbish"? Or is it all the same to him, and depends solely on his understanding of it?
And if it DOES depend only on his understanding of it, what happens if you teach him a word incorrectly?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Missing an 'h' in typographical, first sentence in Description.

I kinda like this, but I'm going to hold my vote for now. I'm not really sure if I like the article for itself or just because I like type.

Why is the typeface blacked out? Didn't quite see a reason for that. If I had to guess though, I'd go with Tahoma.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because you took your time to make a list-like pro and cons, I can answer to them properly:

- It's super meta, and honestly that's the main reason I wrote it. As a little fact, the idea came to me 3 days before the Herman-Fuller euphoria and I considered to make it fit within that, but at the end I went for my original goal.
- I am from a Hispanic country (check the article) and I really wanted to imprint my sign in my skip.
-Yeah, That was totally funny to imagine.

- Honestly, that's part of the skip's nature. I (the Author) constantly change the way I imagine it in my mind. It's up to everybody to picture it on their heads.
- Actually, that was my half answer to allow it to have a written Bible, but that was not gonna happen, I changed it to be a way to avoid it going OTT. Imagine that a complex concept is like several objects linked with a chain (the more words, harder to manifest). And since it is pretty much a weakling, I don't think it can't "lift" a lot.
- Now, originally I imagined it being capable to manifest onomatopoeias, but people reacted really bad to that… I'm thinking to write a suplement one day describing how its nature evolves.

Well, this skip is still up to changes since certain moose is sticking its antlers in my guts to improve it <_<.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Pro:
-A written character who is well aware that he is literally made of words, that is some tasty meta.
-Non-English; the overwhelming majority of our language based skips are in English and I apreciate the change of pace.
-Problem with "weighty" concepts; the sort of wordplay I'm a sucker for.
-Word ballons: that's just a great image. I'd love to see him in a brawl, it'd be like Adam West's Batman. POW! PAMF! BONK! KA-KICK!

Con:
-I can't picture him. My best guess is like a man covered in word tattoos, minus the flesh underneath. But are there gaps? Can you see light through the loop of the "a" in his "manos"?
-Why only isolated words? I can't come up with any fridge-logic reason for why he couldn't just pick single words out of a sentence.
-Only nouns feels like a missed opportunity. A lot of fun could be had with test logs of him manifesting "purple", "chewy", etc. Anything too problematic to write could be hand-waved away with the previously established "weight limit". "Love", for example, would be waaaay too big a concept for him to pull out.

On balance, I'll up-vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Liked this in chat, like it now. Nice first skip, "father." I mean, FaustoV…

+1 for being good at meta (and fixing some problems that held my vote at neutral beforehand.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Moose told me about this and I will work on it tomorrow. I will use the "I want to see what happens" card. And no, I thought about it before scantron mentioned it :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: My guess? They wanted to see what would happen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's super interesting, but I'm not quite sure why the Foundation decided to teach him English. That's honestly my only complaint about this. There needs to be a reason for it that makes sense. Translation obviously isn't an issue since all he needs is a translator who's bilingual in English and Spanish.

Regardless, +1 for this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Fixed all that except the footnote… Holy hell bro, I am having problems thinking how to give a place to the footnote on the paragraph without making it super awkward or something.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I also added a new footnote explaining what an "isolated" word would be.

Gotta be in the descriiiiiption. Can't just have it in footnotes and test logs. Harder, yes, but you gotta do it. The interview helps — a technique often used is to give the Jargon version in the Description, and the colloquial description in the interview.

Some typos, listing here for the benefit of others:

What is the… Heaviest word you ever tried to manifest?

"heaviest" is lowercase.

It just didnt move at all.

*didn't

May God bless your tales brother.

Need comma after 'tales'.

Downvote removed, will reconsider later when fixes are done. I like the concept either way though!

item.title: Took a lot to answer you.
item.content:encoded: When You see those A's, G's, C's and T's, it means that's literally what you see. They are not contracted because that's how the skip's DNA looks like.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok, since I don't have a lot of time I will edit this thread to point everything I fixed.

As per moose's suggestion, I changed the bible to be just an audiobook. Although it's not how I imagined it at first, seeing a living literary concept hear the bible though audiphones is still a bit ironic.

SCP-1516's anatomy is always labeled with the simplest possible term for the body part.

Now I changed it into

Each part of SCP-1516's anatomy is composed of the Spanish word deformed to fit the shape of that part of the body.

I hope in this way it will be more clear.

You constantly shift tense throughout, like "has claimed," "claimed", and "claims" in test logs. Clean this up.

Fixed. Now is just present perfect tense.

Whenever SCP-1516 enters in direct contact with a single, written concept…

I changed that for this.

Whenever SCP-1516 enters in direct contact with a single noun written on any suitable surface,

I also added a new footnote explaining what an "isolated" word would be.

SCP-1516: He was a lovely man, but he had problems focusing in a single subject. Nothing more to say.

Vasquez: Then we are done here.

I added a bit more to the interview, I hope I didnt fall into anything silly.

At ████/██/██ was approved to receive an English course 3 times a week.

I found a way to fix this one, but I need to consult it with other people first.

Also, I fixed all the capitalization issues and typos I found by my own. If you find a typo, don't hesistate to tell me via PM.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Love this. In addition to being well written, it's (to my knowledge) a genuinely new idea, a rare bird indeed.

The bible thing makes me queasy, but I'm not sure I dislike it. I dig your explanation, that some things are too heavy to lift, which works for the subject since he seems to be a religious fellow. I still can't help feeling that the Foundation wouldn't give him ANY books unless they were SURE nothing would come of it, but that's not enough to stop me from upvoting.

Nice job. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1

I like this SCP. A lot.

I'm with the others on the Bible bit. It's not that I don't think you should have it in there (You should, I believe I see where you are going with it, and its your skip.) but you need to have a slightly more cohesive containment structure if you say, "Don't give him destructive words," and then you give him a Bible in the next sentence. Can he understand pictures? Perhaps you can give him a children's picture Bible. Is he maybe limited to pulling things off of a certain type of material or paper? Like he can't transmute words written on leather, or plastic? Because then you could give him a bible with plastic/leather pages.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the concept and the execution allot.
I'm kinda ambivalent about the interview. On one hand, I'm not the biggest fan of meta. On the other hand, I did get a little thrill when it was confirmed that we were the god reading it. In the end, it really doesn't detract too much from my enjoyment.
Regarding the Bible thing, I'd say its a clash between paranoia and curiosity:
On the one hand, there are all the possible things that could go wrong (everything that has already been pointed out). How would they know that he can't effect it before giving it to him? Even if he explains his inability, how can they trust him fully? Even if they do trust him, how do they know that his own understanding of his capabilities is complete? The same logic behind avoiding cross contamination would apply.
On the other hand, the questions raised would be intriguing. Why can't he affect the Bible? What does it actually mean for it to be too heavy for him to affect it? If you were to say, isolate a passage from the Bible that contains no dangerous words, would it retain that weight? What other works have that weight?
Basically, the Bible feels like a hook, something that the Foundation itself would want to follow up on, but hasn't.

On the gripping hand, they could just give him the Bible in audiobook format and bypass the whole mess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's so meta, even this comment is being read by gods.

I actually like it, although it kinda bugs me that the title isn't capitalized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Reminds me of that one kids show, Word World.
It's alright, kind of interesting. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the implied fourth wall breakage. Or maybe I just like the implication that I am a god.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Oh man, you just saved my head with the audiobook suggestion. I will fix everything you pointed tomorrow (sorry, but I really don't have time enough now and my eyes are just no helping me at all). You know, I really wanted to receive this when I posted the draft in the forum it (besides a few comments, the draft got awkwardly ignored). Oh well, I really wanted to post a flawless article, but this is really far from that.

And about Jhon… Not gonna say it on the article, but you can headcanon that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
When it's viewed from a closer perspective

Cut 'it's'.

SCP-1516's anatomy is always labeled with the simplest possible term for the body part.

Unclear. Are the words deformed to fit the shapes of body parts? Or is 1516 basically a stick figure made of words until you look more closely? Confusing. The test logs imply deformation, but it should be clarified in the Description.

it appears to be unable to control it in any form.

"form" is the wrong word.

Result: Upon contact, the word suddenly detached itself, grew in size and deformed to form a tridimensional couch.

You constantly shift tense throughout, like "has claimed," "claimed", and "claims" in test logs. Clean this up.

Whenever SCP-1516 enters in direct contact with a single, written concept

Unclear. The word has to be written… by itself? Is it sufficient to not be part of a sentence — like, would a list of words work? Also, what does a 'concept' mean here? You claim you mean 'noun' in the discussion thread, but that's not what you said. And none of your examples are concepts, just objects.

Result: No change. SCP-1516 claims it can't affect anything beyond an isolated word at once.

This should be in the Description. See above. And it's still unclear. How isolated is "isolated"?

SCP-1516: He was a lovely man, but he had problems focusing in a single subject. Nothing more to say.

Vasquez: Then we are done here.

This feels like the interview ended because the author really wanted to end it, not because the conversation would realistically have gone this way.

At ████/██/██ was approved to receive an English course 3 times a week.

holy shit wait what

They decided to give the SCP a massive power boost for literally no reason? Holy shit, man. At least attempt to justify this! That's just stunning.

Also, regarding the Bible issue — that's complete nonsense. You also have to justify it. The Foundation has no idea what the full extent of this creature's powers are, or how much they may evolve. Just based on the article and nothing else, he could conceivably rip words out and make them come to life. There is essentially no possible way you can justify giving him any book without completely rewriting the article.

How about this: Give him an audiobook copy of the Bible (doesn't matter the method so long as text is not involved with the audio player). He can just listen to it all the time, and there's no risk because it's just spoken word. It doesn't have the visual impact of him reading a book, but it wouldn't require rewriting the entire article.

You could also make his favorite passage the opening of John because I am subtle like a rockslide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I do like this a lot. I'm not, uh, opposed to giving it a bible, but perhaps using a form from which it can't take words would be better. Also, the constant use of contractions threw me off - I feel that the article would be more on-tone if the contracted words were expanded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
My eyes are messing with me right now1, so I can't properly fix those typos. And about the bible, if all the negative is around it, I might take it off. Still, I'm not going to be happy with the change.

Footnotes
1. It's the result of not wearing glasses when you really need to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It just seems very dangerous to give it a Bible.
The Foundation is neither cruel nor kind but cold. They aren't just going to toss a book full of highly dangerous words (angel, demon, brimstone, fire, wrath, war, apocalypse, ad infinitum) to a word-based SCP. In-universe this is a Euclid-Class and there's not any good reason for the Foundation to risk deleterious consequences just to make one SCP happy.
I get that it can't affect it, (although I don't fully understand why), but does the Foundation know, and, assuming they think they know, would they risk it.
There are also a lot of a couple typos.
This is a nice skip, but I'm downvoting until the typos are patched up and the Bible thing is resolved.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I thought a lot about if allowing it to have or not the bible, but It's my personal touch for the skip. It wouldn't be the same for me with no bible.

P,S: and by "personal touch", I don't mean I'm going to give a bible to any other SCP I would make in the future.

E: I also tried to explain that even with the bible in its hands, it can't affect it in the same way you can't lift your entire house over your shoulder.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: E: fixed the XXXX and several minor details. I'm not sure how many typos are left.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yah I'm with this. Giving it a bible is silly.

I like the rest of it though. I think the rest of it is cute as hell. I would snuggle it. <3

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Even if it can only transform nouns, giving it access to the Bible is still a fucking stupid idea.

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lots of typographical errors, ironically, plus an SCP-XXXX. I think with some editing, and especially an eye towards tone, especially in the test log, could improve this. I do like the central idea, but some of the wording is a bit confusing and could definitely be streamlined.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm missing however it is you changed it to make him unable to affect the bible.

Still, this is a really cool concept. At first i thought it was a Cassie-like thing with words, but it quickly dawned on me how different it is.

One thing i have to wonder about, when it is inspected closer, does its physical form actually change, or is it a perceptive thing limited to the one examining it? It sounds like the former, but the latter made my head hurt enough to wonder.
Also, is there an outer limit to the behavior? As in what's the minimum description to form him that is possible?
I'm also wondering what a mass spectrometer would get out of him.

See, you can tell i like it, because my questions are motivated from curiosity, rather than something being wrong. Very good sign.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nothing. Imagine its effect as a muscle. There are certain things too heavy for it to lift. Oh and about the XK, it can actually provoke one, but not in the way you think (and certainly I won't tell how that would happen since I'm not planning to write it at all).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hm. Although it tickles my brain slightly, I'm still a bit irked as to why it's incapable of messing with the bible. Perhaps it just respects the bible too much or something?

Also, as an aside, I'm curious as to how it affects proper nouns.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Check that last paragraph for typos. "English" when referring to the language should always be capitalized (Spanish, too). You kinda flip back and forth in that last part. I found "tridimensional" a little clunky for word choice, but I suppose it's scientifically accurate.

Once it's cleaned up a bit, I'll happily upvote. I enjoyed this one.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I fixed the article to show it cant affect the Bible. And no, it just affects nouns. It wont bring a rain of sulphur and fire using the Bible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: … So you're risking what could possibly result in an XK because you don't want it to be bored. Certainly you could choose a better book for it to have. Or maybe The Foundation could just preview its reading material and make sure there wasn't any dangerous words beforehand?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I gave it a Bible because I (the Author) wanted it to have something to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: What about inanimate fictional concepts? And you still haven't explained why we're giving it what may very well be one of the most dangerous books on the planet for it. Heck, you could give it the anarchists cookbook and it would just blow a hole in the wall, rather than creating what could possibly be an interdimensional horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
May I ask you in what way this can explain more?

Whenever SCP-1516 enters in direct contact with a single, written concept, said word will detach itself from the paper and will deform to fit a functional object depicting the previous concept of the word (hereby called SCP-1516-1).

Actually, I should ponit it is tridimensional. Fixed

I fixed the second point.

And for the last point, if it affects the word "dragon" it will be the same thing that happened to "dog".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can deduce what 1516-1 is, but you need to state what it is directly.

Also, refusing to give him access to dangerous words, then giving him a flipping bible is really fricking stupid. The bible has a ton of words that could be construed as dangerous. I don't want to know what he's going to do with words like brimstone, or god. And for god's sake, don't let him get ahold of any demon names.

downvoted.

Edit: also, what about fictional concepts? could you make a a magic wand, or a dragon?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ok! After over 2 years lurking this palce, I finally wrote something. Special thanks to Tuomey, Athena_Grey (so many questions!) and Vivax. If I forgot your name, let me remind you how bad I am with names. I am damn nervous for this. Enjoy!
